result:
I'll write about something that's been on my mind lately: the peculiar way we experience time, and how our relationship with it shifts as we age.

---

**The Elasticity of Time**

When I was seven, summer lasted approximately forever. I remember lying on the warm concrete of our backyard, watching ants carry impossible loads across vast territories that were, in reality, perhaps three feet of cracked patio. Those afternoons stretched like taffy, sweet and endless, each day a complete universe with its own geography and weather and small dramas. A single June morning contained enough experience to fill a novel, or so it felt.

Now, in my thirties, I blink and it's March. I remember New Year's vaguely, like a party I left early. The weeks accumulate in my calendar like leaves against a fence, and I find myself startled by the evidence of time's passage: the child who was in diapers is reading chapter books, the sapling I planted needs pruning, my own face in the mirror has begun its quiet negotiations with gravity.

This isn't merely poetic observation. There's actual physics and biology here, though we experience it as feeling. When you're five, a year is twenty percent of your entire conscious existence. When you're fifty, it's two percent. The math is brutal and inescapable. Our brains measure duration against the total library of our memories, and as that library expands, each new volume constitutes a smaller fraction of the whole.

But there's something else happening too, something about attention itself.

Children are mostly present. This isn't virtue; it's necessity. The world is still teaching itself to them, and they must pay close attention because everything is new, therefore potentially important, therefore potentially dangerous or delicious or both. The brain of a child is a voracious pattern-recognition machine, burning glucose at extraordinary rates, laying down dense networks of memory because it doesn't yet know what's worth remembering and what isn't.

Adults run on autopilot. We've seen this before—the commute, the grocery store, the Tuesday meeting, the evening routine. The brain conserves energy by predicting rather than perceiving, filling in the expected details rather than constructing experience from scratch. We arrive home with no memory of the drive. We eat dinner while planning tomorrow, or rehashing yesterday, or scrolling through the infinite elsewhere of our phones. The present moment, when we actually inhabit it, becomes rare as a eclipse.

And here's the cruel irony: the less present we are, the faster time seems to go, and the faster time seems to go, the more anxious we become about its passing, which drives us further into abstraction, into planning and worrying and reminiscing, which makes us less present, which makes time go faster still.

I've been experimenting with breaking this cycle, though "experimenting" suggests more rigor than I've managed. Mostly I just try to notice. Walking to the train, I attempt to actually see the particular quality of light on the buildings, the specific cold of this January morning versus some generic idea of winter. I cook sometimes without podcasts, without music, just the rhythm of knife on board, the transformation of ingredients, the particular steam rising from a pot. These moments don't slow time objectively, but they change its texture. They become memorable, which is perhaps the same thing, from the perspective of a life.

There's a concept in philosophy called "temporal depth"—the felt sense of how far back the past extends and how far forward the future reaches. Children have shallow temporal depth. The past is yesterday and maybe last Christmas, the future is this afternoon and possibly summer. This shallowness isn't impoverishment; it's intensity. Everything that exists presses against the present moment with tremendous force.

Adults develop deep temporal depth, and it's a mixed blessing. We can plan for retirement, can learn from ancient history, can understand ourselves as part of long stories. But this depth also dilutes the present. The moment we're actually living in becomes thin as paper, sandwiched between thick volumes of memory and anticipation.

I'm trying to cultivate what I think of as "selective shallowness"—the ability to temporarily collapse my temporal depth, to be more childlike in my attention without being childish in my understanding. It's harder than it sounds. The mind wants to narrate, to connect, to contextualize. Simply seeing a tree as a tree, without thinking about climate change or the tree's species or the last time I saw a similar tree or what I'll be doing after I pass it—this requires something like mental discipline, or perhaps mental surrender.

The pandemic years complicated everyone's relationship with time. For many, they stretched interminably, full of fear and boredom and grief. For others, they compressed to nothing, whole seasons lost to blur. I experienced both, alternating by week or even by hour. What I remember most clearly are the small ruptures in the sameness: the particular evening I sat on my fire escape and watched a man play saxophone on the empty street below, the week I learned to bake sourdough and actually paid attention to the living culture in my kitchen, the first hug I gave after months of isolation, so strange and necessary it felt almost religious.

These moments stand out because they were attended to, because something about their circumstances forced me out of automatic processing and into actual experience. The saxophonist was unexpected, the sourdough required patience I wasn't accustomed to, the hug broke a taboo that had become second nature. Novelty, difficulty, significance—these are the conditions under which memory forms, and memory is, ultimately, how we know we've lived.

I'm not sure what to do with all of this, except to keep noticing, keep trying to trick myself into presence. I fail constantly. I spent twenty minutes this morning in a spiral of abstract anxiety about work I haven't started yet, while my coffee went cold and the particular morning light shifted into something else. But I also spent five minutes watching a squirrel navigate a fence with ridiculous determination, and I was actually there for that, and it was somehow enough, or almost enough, or at least a beginning.

The physicists tell us that time isn't really what we think it is anyway—that past and future may be equally real, that the present moment has no special ontological status, that our experience of time's arrow emerges from entropy rather than any fundamental feature of reality. This should be liberating, maybe. If time doesn't fundamentally flow, then my anxiety about its passing is based on a kind of illusion. But I don't experience it as liberating. I experience it as one more layer of strangeness in an already strange existence, one more thing to think about when I should be looking at the actual squirrel.

What I want, I think, is not more time but more time-fullness—the sense that the time I have is actually inhabited rather than merely spent. The ants on the patio didn't know they were making a memory that would last decades. They were just working, just alive, just present in their ant-specific way. I can't go back to that kind of unselfconscious presence. I know too much, have lived too long, have too many layers of temporal depth to shed. But I can try, sometimes, to approximate it. To let a moment be enough. To trust that attending to the small thing in front of me is not a waste of the limited time remaining, but perhaps the only way to make that time matter at all.

The light is changing now, afternoon sliding toward evening. I've been writing for longer than I expected, and shorter than it feels. Time does that too, contracts and expands in the experiencing, regardless of the clock. I started this wanting to say something true about how we live in duration, and I'm not sure I've succeeded. But I was here for the attempt, mostly, and that will have to be sufficient. The squirrel has moved on to whatever squirrel business remains. The coffee is cold. The year continues its acceleration, and I continue my small resistance, paying attention when I can, letting the moments accumulate into something like a life.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
